---
title: "celltrack EDA — Biohub Cell Tracking During Development"
subtitle: "What the real training & test data actually look like"
author: "celltrack"
date: last-modified
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    fig-width: 8
    fig-height: 4.5
    embed-resources: true
    theme: cosmo
jupyter: python3
execute:
  warning: false
  cache: true
---

Exploratory analysis of the downloaded competition data, verifying the
assumptions baked into `celltrack` (voxel scale, match gate, sparse-GT metric)
against what is actually on disk. Every number here is measured live from
`data/`; nothing is hard-coded.

The takeaways feed directly into the modelling strategy:

- the **anisotropic voxel scale** and **7 µm match gate** the tracker/metric rely on,
- how **sparse** the ground truth is (why the metric ignores unmatched nodes),
- how far cells **move per frame** (does the 7 µm gate actually cover real motion?),
- how common **divisions** are (the core-IP target), and
- that the **4 test images have offline ground truth** (a real local metric).

```{python}
# | label: setup
from __future__ import annotations

import json
from pathlib import Path

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

from celltrack.constants import MATCH_MAX_UM, VOXEL_SCALE_UM
from celltrack.data.geff import read_geff
from celltrack.data.io import open_dataset, read_volume
from celltrack.eval.metric import score


# Resolve the repo root whether rendered from notebooks/ or the repo root.
def _find_root() -> Path:
    for base in (Path.cwd(), *Path.cwd().parents):
        if (base / "data" / "train").is_dir():
            return base
    raise FileNotFoundError("Could not locate data/train from the working directory.")


ROOT = _find_root()
TRAIN, TEST = ROOT / "data" / "train", ROOT / "data" / "test"

plt.rcParams.update({"figure.dpi": 110, "axes.grid": True, "grid.alpha": 0.3})
C_44b6, C_6bba = "#4C72B0", "#DD8452"  # per-embryo colours used throughout
print(f"repo root: {ROOT}")
print(f"VOXEL_SCALE_UM (z, y, x) = {VOXEL_SCALE_UM}  |  MATCH_MAX_UM = {MATCH_MAX_UM} µm")
```

## 1. Data inventory

```{python}
# | label: inventory
train_geff = sorted(TRAIN.glob("*.geff"))
train_zarr = sorted(TRAIN.glob("*.zarr"))
test_zarr = sorted(TEST.glob("*.zarr"))


def embryo(name: str) -> str:
    return name.split("_")[0]


train_names = [p.name[: -len(".geff")] for p in train_geff]
inv = pd.DataFrame({"dataset": train_names})
inv["embryo"] = inv["dataset"].map(embryo)

print(f"train: {len(train_geff)} .geff + {len(train_zarr)} .zarr pairs")
print(f"test:  {len(test_zarr)} .zarr images (no shipped GT dir)")
print("\ntrain datasets per embryo:")
print(inv["embryo"].value_counts().to_string())
```

Two embryos, distinguished by the name prefix (`44b6_*`, `6bba_*`). Every train
dataset is an image + ground-truth pair; the test folder holds images only.

## 2. Image geometry & voxel scale

Image metadata comes straight from each `zarr.json` (no pixel reads), so this is
cheap to run across all datasets.

```{python}
# | label: geometry
def image_meta(zarr_path: Path) -> dict:
    root = json.loads((zarr_path / "zarr.json").read_text())
    arr = json.loads((zarr_path / "0" / "zarr.json").read_text())
    ms = root["attributes"]["multiscales"][0]
    scale = ms["datasets"][0]["coordinateTransformations"][0]["scale"]
    q = root["attributes"].get("image_statistics", {}).get("quantiles", {})
    return {
        "dataset": zarr_path.name[: -len(".zarr")],
        "shape": tuple(arr["shape"]),
        "dtype": arr["data_type"],
        "scale_t": scale[0],
        "scale_z": scale[1],
        "scale_y": scale[2],
        "scale_x": scale[3],
        **{f"q{k}": v for k, v in q.items()},
    }


geom = pd.DataFrame(image_meta(p) for p in train_zarr + test_zarr)
print("unique image shapes:", geom["shape"].value_counts().to_dict())
print("unique dtypes:      ", geom["dtype"].unique().tolist())
print("unique (z,y,x) scales:")
print(geom[["scale_z", "scale_y", "scale_x"]].drop_duplicates().to_string(index=False))
```

Every image is identical in geometry: **`(T=100, Z=64, Y=256, X=256)` `uint16`**
(~800 MB each — modest, not terabyte-scale). The physical voxel scale matches
`VOXEL_SCALE_UM` exactly: z is **4×** coarser than x/y, which is why all distances
must be computed in scaled µm, never raw voxels.

### Intensity distributions

Each `zarr.json` embeds per-dataset intensity quantiles, so we can compare
brightness across all datasets without touching a single voxel.

```{python}
# | label: intensity
# | fig-cap: "Per-dataset intensity quantiles (log scale). Wide spread ⇒ detection needs per-image normalisation."
qcols = [c for c in geom.columns if c.startswith("q")]
qlevels = [float(c[1:]) for c in qcols]
geom["embryo"] = geom["dataset"].map(embryo).where(
    geom["dataset"].isin(train_names), other="test"
)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4.5))
for _, row in geom.iterrows():
    col = {"44b6": C_44b6, "6bba": C_6bba, "test": "#999999"}[row["embryo"]]
    ax.plot(qlevels, [row[c] for c in qcols], color=col, alpha=0.25, lw=0.8)
ax.set_yscale("log")
ax.set_xlabel("quantile")
ax.set_ylabel("intensity (uint16)")
ax.set_title("Intensity quantile curves, one line per dataset")
handles = [
    plt.Line2D([], [], color=C_44b6, label="44b6"),
    plt.Line2D([], [], color=C_6bba, label="6bba"),
    plt.Line2D([], [], color="#999999", label="test"),
]
ax.legend(handles=handles, title="embryo")
plt.show()

med = geom.groupby("embryo")[["q0.1", "q0.9", "q0.99", "q1.0"]].median()
print("median intensity quantiles by embryo:")
print(med.round(0).to_string())
```

Intensity ranges vary widely between datasets (background ~15–100, bright nuclei
up to ~4000), and the two embryos differ. **Detection must normalise per image**
— a fixed global threshold will not transfer.

## 3. Ground-truth graph structure

Now scan every `.geff` into a `TrackGraph` and summarise. This reads only the
graph stores (small), not the images.

```{python}
# | label: gt-scan
def gt_summary(geff_path: Path) -> dict:
    g, est = read_geff(geff_path)
    tps = g.timepoints()
    # frame-to-frame displacement along each edge, in scaled µm
    disps = []
    for u, v in g.edges():
        nu, nv = g.node(u), g.node(v)
        disps.append(float(np.linalg.norm(nu.scaled_coord() - nv.scaled_coord())))
    disps = np.asarray(disps) if disps else np.array([np.nan])
    return {
        "dataset": geff_path.name[: -len(".geff")],
        "embryo": embryo(geff_path.name),
        "n_nodes": g.num_nodes(),
        "n_edges": g.num_edges(),
        "n_divisions": len(g.divisions()),
        "est_nodes": est,
        "t_min": min(tps),
        "t_max": max(tps),
        "n_timepoints": len(tps),
        "disp_median_um": float(np.nanmedian(disps)),
        "disp_p99_um": float(np.nanpercentile(disps, 99)),
        "disp_max_um": float(np.nanmax(disps)),
    }


gt = pd.DataFrame(gt_summary(p) for p in train_geff)
gt["gt_coverage"] = gt["n_nodes"] / gt["est_nodes"]  # fraction of cells traced
gt.describe().loc[["min", "50%", "max"]][
    ["n_nodes", "n_edges", "n_divisions", "est_nodes", "gt_coverage", "n_timepoints"]
].round(3)
```

```{python}
# | label: gt-dist
# | fig-cap: "Distribution of GT graph sizes across the 199 training datasets."
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(11, 3.4))
for ax, col, title in zip(
    axes,
    ["n_nodes", "n_edges", "n_divisions"],
    ["GT nodes / dataset", "GT edges / dataset", "GT divisions / dataset"],
):
    for emb, c in [("44b6", C_44b6), ("6bba", C_6bba)]:
        ax.hist(gt.loc[gt.embryo == emb, col], bins=25, alpha=0.6, color=c, label=emb)
    ax.set_title(title)
    ax.set_xlabel(col)
axes[0].set_ylabel("datasets")
axes[0].legend(title="embryo")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

## 4. The ground truth is *sparse*

This is the single most important fact for the metric. The GT is a hand-traced
subset of cells — `estimated_number_of_nodes` records the *true* dense cell count.

```{python}
# | label: sparsity
# | fig-cap: "Traced GT nodes vs the estimated true cell count. Points sit far below the diagonal."
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 5))
for emb, c in [("44b6", C_44b6), ("6bba", C_6bba)]:
    sub = gt[gt.embryo == emb]
    ax.scatter(sub["est_nodes"], sub["n_nodes"], s=18, alpha=0.6, color=c, label=emb)
lim = [gt["est_nodes"].min(), gt["est_nodes"].max()]
ax.plot(lim, lim, "k--", lw=1, alpha=0.5, label="1:1 (fully traced)")
ax.plot(lim, [x * 0.1 for x in lim], "k:", lw=1, alpha=0.5, label="10% traced")
ax.set_xlabel("estimated_number_of_nodes (dense true count)")
ax.set_ylabel("GT nodes actually traced")
ax.set_title("GT coverage — the annotation is a sparse subset")
ax.legend()
plt.show()

print(f"median GT coverage: {gt['gt_coverage'].median():.1%} of cells traced")
print(f"range: {gt['gt_coverage'].min():.1%} – {gt['gt_coverage'].max():.1%}")
```

Only a few percent of cells are traced. **Consequences the metric already
encodes (do not "fix"):**

- predicted edges touching *unmatched* nodes are **ignored**, not counted as false
  positives — otherwise every correctly-detected-but-unlabelled cell would be
  punished;
- an explicit over-prediction penalty `raw × (n_est / n_pred)` kicks in only when
  you predict *more* nodes than the dense estimate.

This means detection recall on unlabelled cells is essentially free; what is
scored is the **linking** among cells that happen to be traced.

## 5. Temporal coverage

```{python}
# | label: temporal
# | fig-cap: "GT covers t=0 onward but often stops before the 100-frame image ends."
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 3.4))
ax.hist(gt["t_max"], bins=range(0, 101, 5), color="#55A868", alpha=0.8)
ax.axvline(99, color="k", ls="--", lw=1, label="last image frame (t=99)")
ax.set_xlabel("last GT timepoint (t_max)")
ax.set_ylabel("datasets")
ax.set_title("GT temporal extent (images always have T=100)")
ax.legend()
plt.show()

print(f"t_min is always 0: {(gt['t_min'] == 0).all()}")
print(gt["t_max"].describe()[["min", "50%", "max"]].round(0).to_string())
```

GT always starts at `t=0`; its end varies (median 99, some as early as 50).
Predictions may be made on all 100 frames, but are only scored where GT exists.

## 6. Cell motion — does the 7 µm gate cover real displacement?

The tracker's association cost and the metric both gate node matches at
`MATCH_MAX_UM = 7 µm`. That gate governs *matching predictions to GT*, but the
frame-to-frame displacement of a real cell is the closest physical analogue for
"how far a link reaches". If cells routinely move further than 7 µm per frame,
linking is genuinely hard.

```{python}
# | label: motion
# | fig-cap: "Frame-to-frame displacement along GT edges (scaled µm), pooled across all datasets."
disp_all = []
for p in train_geff:
    g, _ = read_geff(p)
    for u, v in g.edges():
        nu, nv = g.node(u), g.node(v)
        disp_all.append(float(np.linalg.norm(nu.scaled_coord() - nv.scaled_coord())))
disp_all = np.asarray(disp_all)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4))
ax.hist(disp_all, bins=80, color="#8172B3", alpha=0.85)
ax.axvline(MATCH_MAX_UM, color="crimson", ls="--", lw=1.5, label=f"MATCH_MAX_UM = {MATCH_MAX_UM} µm")
ax.set_xlabel("GT edge displacement (µm / frame)")
ax.set_ylabel("edges")
ax.set_title("How far cells move between consecutive frames")
ax.legend()
plt.show()

pct_over = (disp_all > MATCH_MAX_UM).mean()
print(f"edges: {len(disp_all):,}")
for p in (50, 90, 99, 100):
    print(f"  p{p:<3d} displacement: {np.percentile(disp_all, p):5.2f} µm")
print(f"fraction of GT links longer than {MATCH_MAX_UM} µm: {pct_over:.2%}")
```

Most cells move well under the gate, but the tail matters: the fraction of links
exceeding 7 µm quantifies how many true links a pure nearest-neighbour tracker
within the gate can never recover. A motion-aware / gap-closing tracker (the core
IP) is what reaches those.

## 7. Divisions — the core-IP target

Half the metric is the Division Jaccard, and the baseline tracker emits **zero**
divisions. So division recall is pure upside.

```{python}
# | label: divisions
n_with = int((gt["n_divisions"] > 0).sum())
print(f"datasets with >=1 division: {n_with} / {len(gt)}")
print(f"total divisions across train: {int(gt['n_divisions'].sum())}")
print(f"divisions per dataset (nonzero): "
      f"median {int(gt.loc[gt.n_divisions > 0, 'n_divisions'].median())}, "
      f"max {int(gt['n_divisions'].max())}")

# When do divisions happen? Collect the timepoint of every parent node.
div_times = []
for p in train_geff:
    g, _ = read_geff(p)
    for d in g.divisions():
        div_times.append(g.node(d).t)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 3.4))
ax.hist(div_times, bins=range(0, 101, 5), color="#C44E52", alpha=0.85)
ax.set_xlabel("timepoint of division (parent node t)")
ax.set_ylabel("division events")
ax.set_title("When divisions occur over the movie")
plt.show()
```

## 8. The test set has offline ground truth

The four test images are **byte-identical copies of same-named datasets in
`data/train`**, which ship `.geff` GT. So we can compute the exact competition
metric on the real test images locally — a strong leaderboard proxy (with the
usual caveat: don't overfit to just four datasets, and the Kaggle LB still scores
against its own held-out sparse GT).

```{python}
# | label: test-gt
test_names = [p.name[: -len(".zarr")] for p in test_zarr]
rows = []
for n in test_names:
    tr = TRAIN / f"{n}.geff"
    rows.append(
        {
            "dataset": n,
            "in_train": tr.is_dir(),
            **{k: v for k, v in gt_summary(tr).items() if k in ("n_nodes", "n_edges", "n_divisions", "est_nodes")},
        }
    )
pd.DataFrame(rows)
```

```{python}
# | label: local-metric-demo
# Oracle: scoring the GT graphs against themselves must yield a perfect score,
# which confirms the metric + loaders agree on the real files.
graphs, est = {}, {}
for n in test_names:
    g, e = read_geff(TRAIN / f"{n}.geff")
    graphs[n], est[n] = g, e

res = score(graphs, graphs, est_nodes=est)
print("Local metric oracle on the 4 test datasets (GT vs GT):")
print(f"  Edge Jaccard:     {res.edge_jaccard:.4f}")
print(f"  Division Jaccard: {res.division_jaccard:.4f}")
print(f"  Combined:         {res.combined:.4f}")
print("\nWorkflow for a real submission:")
print("  celltrack eval submission.csv data/train --pred-only --per-dataset")
```

## 9. Visual check — image with GT overlay

Finally, confirm the GT centroids actually land on nuclei. We read a **single
timepoint** of one volume (streaming, never the whole array) and overlay the GT
nodes at that frame on a max-intensity projection over z.

```{python}
# | label: overlay
# | fig-cap: "Max-z projection of one timepoint with GT node centroids (yellow) overlaid."
demo = test_names[-1]  # a dataset that also has divisions
g, _ = read_geff(TRAIN / f"{demo}.geff")
# pick the timepoint with the most GT nodes for a fuller picture
best_t = max(g.timepoints(), key=lambda t: len(g.nodes_at_time(t)))
nodes = g.nodes_at_time(best_t)

arr = open_dataset(TEST, demo)
vol = np.asarray(read_volume(arr, best_t))  # (Z, Y, X), one frame only
proj = vol.max(axis=0)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 6))
ax.imshow(proj, cmap="gray", vmax=np.percentile(proj, 99.5))
ax.scatter(
    [n.x for n in nodes], [n.y for n in nodes],
    s=40, facecolors="none", edgecolors="yellow", linewidths=1.2,
)
ax.set_title(f"{demo}  t={best_t}  ({len(nodes)} GT nodes)")
ax.set_xlabel("x")
ax.set_ylabel("y")
ax.grid(False)
plt.show()
```

## Summary

| Fact | Value | Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Image shape / dtype | `(100, 64, 256, 256)` uint16, ~800 MB | fits in RAM; stream by choice, not necessity |
| Voxel scale (z, y, x) µm | `(1.625, 0.40625, 0.40625)` | anisotropic; always work in scaled µm |
| GT coverage | ~few % of cells traced | metric ignores unmatched nodes; detection recall is cheap |
| GT edge displacement | most < 7 µm, with a tail | NN-within-gate misses long links → motion-aware tracker wins |
| Divisions | present in many datasets, baseline emits 0 | half the metric is pure upside |
| Test GT | 4 test images duplicated in train w/ `.geff` | real local metric via `eval --pred-only` |

The verified constants (`VOXEL_SCALE_UM`, `MATCH_MAX_UM`) and the sparse-GT
metric rules in `celltrack` all hold up against the real data.
